steps.title: Primary Sources of Technical Information
steps.objective: Identify the primary sources of technical information used by electricians.
anchorFacts: On any electrical project, you rely on two main site-specific documents: Specifications and Drawings. A Specification is a written document that acts as the master list for the job. Its primary purpose is material selection; it details the exact types, grades, and quality of materials required, such as 'all conduit must be galvanized steel.'

Drawings provide the visual 'wiring system information.' While the specification tells you what to buy, the drawings show you where to put it. They illustrate the physical routes cables must take through a building and the exact locations of accessories like sockets or distribution boards. Using these two sources together ensures you install the correct equipment in the correct place, preventing expensive mistakes or safety issues caused by using the wrong materials for the environment.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking that all technical information is found in a single document.
Think about which document is a written list of materials and which is a visual map of the installation.

steps.title: Regulatory and Manufacturer Data
steps.objective: Explain the role of BS 7671, the On-Site Guide, and manufacturer data.
anchorFacts: Beyond site-specific plans, electricians must follow national safety standards. BS 7671 is the 'Requirements for Electrical Installations,' commonly known as the 'Regs.' This is the primary rulebook for electrical safety and compliance in the UK. Because the full Regs are very detailed, electricians often use the On-Site Guide (OSG), which is a simplified handbook containing the most common rules and tables for everyday installation work.

You must also consult Manufacturer Data. This is the specific instruction manual provided by the maker of a component, such as a circuit breaker or a thermostat. It contains critical technical limits, like the exact torque setting for tightening a terminal screw. Following manufacturer data is essential because it ensures the component functions as designed; ignoring a specific torque setting can lead to loose connections and fire risks, even if you followed general regulations.
misconceptionsToWatch: Believing manufacturer data overrides BS 7671 safety requirements.
Consider the difference between a national safety rulebook (BS 7671) and the specific instructions for a single piece of equipment.

steps.title: Purpose of Technical Sources
steps.objective: State the specific purpose of different technical information sources per AC 2.1.
anchorFacts: Every source of technical information has a specific role in a project. We use Specifications for material selection to ensure the installation meets the quality standards required by the designer. We use Drawings to obtain wiring system information, such as cable routes and equipment positions. Sometimes, you must also consider 'Client Needs.' These are specific requests from the customer that might not be on the main drawings yet, such as a preference for a specific style of decorative faceplate.

Other sources include Guidance Notes, which provide deep technical explanations on complex topics like earthing or protection against electric shock. Finally, the Unite Union Book (also known as the National Working Rules) provides information on site conduct, grading of electricians, and employment conditions. Knowing which source to check allows you to find answers quickly, whether you are looking for a cable route or checking your rights on a construction site.
misconceptionsToWatch: Assuming 'Client needs' are always captured fully in the standard drawings.
Match the source to its job: materials come from specs, routes come from drawings, and specific requests come from the client.

steps.title: Functional Drawing Types
steps.objective: Distinguish between block diagrams, schematics, and wiring diagrams.
anchorFacts: Electricians use different drawing types depending on the task. A Block Diagram is a simple overview using labeled boxes and single lines to show how the main parts of a system relate to each other. It provides the 'big picture' without any detail. A Schematic Diagram shows the logical flow and operation of a circuit. It tells you how the system works and the sequence of components, but it does not show where the parts are physically located in the building.

A Wiring Diagram is much more detailed. It shows the actual physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring between components. If you are trying to understand the logic of a motor starter, you look at the schematic. If you are physically connecting the wires to the terminals inside that starter, you must use the wiring diagram. Confusing these two can lead to wiring errors because a schematic is designed for understanding logic, not for physical installation.
misconceptionsToWatch: Confusing a schematic diagram with a physical wiring diagram.
A schematic shows how a system 'thinks' or works, while a wiring diagram shows exactly where the wires go.

steps.title: As-Fitted Drawings and Bar Charts
steps.objective: Recognize the purpose of as-fitted drawings and bar charts in project management.
anchorFacts: Not all technical documents are about the electrical circuit itself. A Bar Chart is a project management tool used for scheduling. It shows the timeline of the project, indicating when specific tasks start and when they must be finished. It helps the electrician coordinate with other trades, like builders or plumbers.

Once the work is finished, As-Fitted Drawings are produced. These are the final versions of the original design drawings, updated to show exactly what was installed. During construction, cable routes often change to avoid obstacles like new pipes or beams. The as-fitted drawing records these changes accurately. This is vital for future maintenance and safety; if a fault occurs years later, the maintenance electrician relies on the as-fitted drawing to find the exact location of hidden cables or junction boxes without having to tear down walls based on outdated original plans.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking as-fitted drawings are identical to the original design drawings.
One document manages the project's time (Bar Chart), while the other records the final 'as-installed' reality (As-Fitted).

steps.title: Worked Example: Selecting the Correct Technical Source
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to select and use the correct technical sources when installing a specific piece of equipment, such as a smart thermostat.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are tasked with installing a new smart thermostat in a residential property. You have the thermostat in its box, a set of site drawings, and your On-Site Guide.

Step 1: Consult the On-Site Guide (BS 7671). You check the OSG to ensure the circuit supplying the heating system has the correct overcurrent protection and that your cable selection meets general safety regulations for the environment.

Step 2: Check the Project Specification. You refer to the written specification to confirm the exact model and finish required by the designer (e.g., 'Smart Thermostat - Polished Chrome finish') to ensure the materials you are about to install match the contract.

Step 3: Consult the Manufacturer Data. You open the manufacturer's instruction leaflet to find the specific torque setting for the terminal screws (e.g., 0.5 Nm) and the internal wiring logic for the 'Call for Heat' terminals. This information is not in the Regs or the site drawings.

Step 4: Use the Wiring Diagram. You look at the detailed wiring diagram provided for the heating system to identify which physical wire colors in the wall box correspond to the power supply and the boiler control loop.

Critical rules:
- Terms must be lesson-specific and grounded in the source.
- Definitions must be short and technically defensible.
- anchorPhrases should be reusable phrases that later phases can teach verbatim or near-verbatim.

terms.simpleDefinition: A written document detailing the exact materials, quality standards, and installation methods required for a project.
terms.anchor: The master list for material selection and quality.
terms.misconception: Thinking specifications are the same as drawings; drawings show where things go, while specifications state what they must be made of.

terms.simpleDefinition: Technical drawings updated after the work is finished to show the exact final location of all equipment and cables.
terms.anchor: The 'map' of what was actually installed.
terms.misconception: Assuming the original design drawings are always accurate; changes often happen during installation that only appear on as-fitted drawings.

terms.simpleDefinition: A drawing that shows the logical flow and operation of a system rather than the physical location of components.
terms.anchor: Shows how the system works, not where it sits.
terms.misconception: Trying to use a schematic to find the physical route of a cable through a building.

terms.simpleDefinition: A detailed drawing showing the physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring between components.
terms.anchor: The guide for making actual physical connections.
terms.misconception: Confusing a wiring diagram with a schematic; a wiring diagram is for connecting, a schematic is for understanding logic.

terms.simpleDefinition: A simple overview using labeled boxes to represent main components and lines to show their relationship.
terms.anchor: The 'big picture' summary of a system.
terms.misconception: Expecting to see individual wire colors or terminal numbers on a block diagram.

terms.simpleDefinition: A project management tool used to schedule tasks and track the progress of work over time.
terms.misconception: Thinking a bar chart contains technical electrical data; it only manages time and sequence.

terms.simpleDefinition: Technical instructions and limits provided by the equipment maker, such as torque settings or temperature ratings.
terms.anchor: The specific 'instruction manual' for a product.
terms.misconception: Believing that general regulations (BS 7671) always override specific manufacturer installation instructions.

terms.simpleDefinition: The national safety standards (Regulations) and the simplified practical handbook for electrical installations.
terms.anchor: The 'rule book' for safety and compliance.
terms.misconception: Thinking the On-Site Guide replaces BS 7671; it is a simplified version for common tasks, not a full replacement.

terms.simpleDefinition: A drawing representing the electrical sequence of components in a circuit, often used for fault finding.
terms.anchor: The electrical path of a single circuit.
terms.misconception: Thinking a circuit diagram shows the physical layout of the room.

anchorPhrases: As-fitted drawings show the final installation
Schematics show logic, wiring diagrams show connections
Manufacturer data for specific component limits
misconceptionTargets: Confusing a schematic with a physical wiring diagram
Assuming design drawings are always updated to as-fitted status
Thinking manufacturer data is optional if you know the regulations
Using a block diagram to find specific terminal connections
Believing specifications and drawings are the same document

teachChecks.title: Primary Sources of Technical Information
teachChecks.objective: Identify the primary sources of technical information used by electricians.
retrievalTextLines: On any electrical project, you rely on two main site-specific documents: Specifications and Drawings. A Specification is a written document that acts as the master list for the job. Its primary purpose is material selection; it details the exact types, grades, and quality of materials required, such as 'all conduit must be galvanized steel.'

Drawings provide the visual 'wiring system information.' While the specification tells you what to buy, the drawings show you where to put it. They illustrate the physical routes cables must take through a building and the exact locations of accessories like sockets or distribution boards. Using these two sources together ensures you install the correct equipment in the correct place, preventing expensive mistakes or safety issues caused by using the wrong materials for the environment.
teachChecks.hint: Think about which document is a written list of materials and which is a visual map of the installation.

teachChecks.title: Regulatory and Manufacturer Data
teachChecks.objective: Explain the role of BS 7671, the On-Site Guide, and manufacturer data.
retrievalTextLines: Beyond site-specific plans, electricians must follow national safety standards. BS 7671 is the 'Requirements for Electrical Installations,' commonly known as the 'Regs.' This is the primary rulebook for electrical safety and compliance in the UK. Because the full Regs are very detailed, electricians often use the On-Site Guide (OSG), which is a simplified handbook containing the most common rules and tables for everyday installation work.

You must also consult Manufacturer Data. This is the specific instruction manual provided by the maker of a component, such as a circuit breaker or a thermostat. It contains critical technical limits, like the exact torque setting for tightening a terminal screw. Following manufacturer data is essential because it ensures the component functions as designed; ignoring a specific torque setting can lead to loose connections and fire risks, even if you followed general regulations.
teachChecks.hint: Consider the difference between a national safety rulebook (BS 7671) and the specific instructions for a single piece of equipment.

teachChecks.title: Purpose of Technical Sources
teachChecks.objective: State the specific purpose of different technical information sources per AC 2.1.
retrievalTextLines: Every source of technical information has a specific role in a project. We use Specifications for material selection to ensure the installation meets the quality standards required by the designer. We use Drawings to obtain wiring system information, such as cable routes and equipment positions. Sometimes, you must also consider 'Client Needs.' These are specific requests from the customer that might not be on the main drawings yet, such as a preference for a specific style of decorative faceplate.

Other sources include Guidance Notes, which provide deep technical explanations on complex topics like earthing or protection against electric shock. Finally, the Unite Union Book (also known as the National Working Rules) provides information on site conduct, grading of electricians, and employment conditions. Knowing which source to check allows you to find answers quickly, whether you are looking for a cable route or checking your rights on a construction site.
teachChecks.hint: Match the source to its job: materials come from specs, routes come from drawings, and specific requests come from the client.

teachChecks.title: Functional Drawing Types
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between block diagrams, schematics, and wiring diagrams.
retrievalTextLines: Electricians use different drawing types depending on the task. A Block Diagram is a simple overview using labeled boxes and single lines to show how the main parts of a system relate to each other. It provides the 'big picture' without any detail. A Schematic Diagram shows the logical flow and operation of a circuit. It tells you how the system works and the sequence of components, but it does not show where the parts are physically located in the building.

A Wiring Diagram is much more detailed. It shows the actual physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring between components. If you are trying to understand the logic of a motor starter, you look at the schematic. If you are physically connecting the wires to the terminals inside that starter, you must use the wiring diagram. Confusing these two can lead to wiring errors because a schematic is designed for understanding logic, not for physical installation.
teachChecks.hint: A schematic shows how a system 'thinks' or works, while a wiring diagram shows exactly where the wires go.

teachChecks.title: As-Fitted Drawings and Bar Charts
teachChecks.objective: Recognize the purpose of as-fitted drawings and bar charts in project management.
retrievalTextLines: Not all technical documents are about the electrical circuit itself. A Bar Chart is a project management tool used for scheduling. It shows the timeline of the project, indicating when specific tasks start and when they must be finished. It helps the electrician coordinate with other trades, like builders or plumbers.

Once the work is finished, As-Fitted Drawings are produced. These are the final versions of the original design drawings, updated to show exactly what was installed. During construction, cable routes often change to avoid obstacles like new pipes or beams. The as-fitted drawing records these changes accurately. This is vital for future maintenance and safety; if a fault occurs years later, the maintenance electrician relies on the as-fitted drawing to find the exact location of hidden cables or junction boxes without having to tear down walls based on outdated original plans.
teachChecks.hint: One document manages the project's time (Bar Chart), while the other records the final 'as-installed' reality (As-Fitted).

Critical rules:
- basicQuestions must contain exactly 3 items.
- Each basic question must test one taught fact only.
- Each basic question answerGuidance must contain 2-5 checkable phrases or ideas.
- Never ask the learner to supply multiple separate facts in one basic question.
- Prefer single-fact or single-term checks that support precise corrective feedback.
- If a question asks for the specific term, name, abbreviation expansion, code letter, or technical label, the first answerGuidance item must be the exact target from the lesson vocabulary.
- If a question defines something and asks for its name or type, answerGuidance must contain the exact technical term that matches that definition.
- For exact-term questions, answerGuidance may include only tight equivalents of that same target. Do not accept loose descriptive paraphrases, topic labels, or nearby concepts from other questions.
- If a question asks what one code letter stands for, answerGuidance must contain only that code-letter expansion word or a tight grammatical variant, not a component name or neighbouring concept.
- Keep each answerGuidance list local to its own question only. Do not copy valid answers from adjacent questions in the same step.
- The deeper question must connect the explanation facts and ask why/how/what-must-be-true.
- deeperAnswerGuidance must contain 3-6 checkable ideas.

teachChecks.title: Primary Sources of Technical Information

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of a Specification document?
What specific type of information is provided by site drawings?
Which site-specific document acts as the master list for the quality and grade of materials?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why must an electrician use both the Specification and the Drawings together rather than relying on just one?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Drawings show the physical location and routes
Specifications define the quality and type of materials
Using only drawings might lead to installing the wrong grade of material
Using only specifications would leave the electrician without a physical layout
Both are required to ensure the correct equipment is in the correct place
teachChecks.hint: Think about which document is a written list of materials and which is a visual map of the installation.

teachChecks.title: Regulatory and Manufacturer Data

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the common name for the BS 7671 regulations?
Which simplified handbook contains common rules and tables for everyday installation work?
Where should an electrician look to find the specific torque setting for a terminal screw?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is following manufacturer data considered essential even if an electrician is already following the general BS 7671 regulations?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Manufacturer data contains specific technical limits for that product
General regulations do not cover specific torque settings for every device
Ensures the component functions exactly as designed
Prevents safety risks like fire from loose connections
Compliance with manufacturer instructions is often a requirement for the warranty and safety
teachChecks.hint: Consider the difference between a national safety rulebook (BS 7671) and the specific instructions for a single piece of equipment.

teachChecks.title: Purpose of Technical Sources

basicQuestions.questionText: Which source should be consulted for specific customer requests that are not yet on the main drawings?
Which document provides information on site conduct and the grading of electricians?
What is the purpose of Guidance Notes in electrical work?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does knowing the specific purpose of each technical source improve efficiency on a construction site?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Allows the electrician to find answers quickly without searching multiple documents
Prevents errors by ensuring the correct document is used for the specific task
Ensures material selection matches the designer's quality standards
Reduces downtime spent waiting for clarification from supervisors
Helps distinguish between safety rules, material lists, and employment rights
teachChecks.hint: Match the source to its job: materials come from specs, routes come from drawings, and specific requests come from the client.

teachChecks.title: Functional Drawing Types

basicQuestions.questionText: Which drawing type uses labeled boxes and single lines to show a simple system overview?
What does a Schematic Diagram primarily show?
Which drawing type shows the actual terminal-to-terminal connections between components?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is a schematic diagram unsuitable for the physical installation of wires in a complex control panel?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Schematics do not show the physical location of components
They focus on the logic of the circuit rather than the physical layout
Wiring diagrams are needed to see specific terminal numbers and connections
Using a schematic for wiring would likely lead to physical connection errors
The schematic is designed for understanding operation or fault finding
teachChecks.hint: A schematic shows how a system 'thinks' or works, while a wiring diagram shows exactly where the wires go.

teachChecks.title: As-Fitted Drawings and Bar Charts

basicQuestions.questionText: Which project management tool is used to show the timeline and schedule of tasks?
What is the purpose of an As-Fitted drawing?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why are as-fitted drawings more important for a maintenance electrician than the original design drawings?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Original designs often change during the actual build to avoid obstacles
As-fitted drawings show the real, final location of hidden cables
Maintenance requires accurate information to avoid damaging services behind walls
Original plans may be outdated and inaccurate by the time the job is finished
They provide a reliable record for future fault finding and safety
teachChecks.hint: One document manages the project's time (Bar Chart), while the other records the final 'as-installed' reality (As-Fitted).

      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Beyond site-specific plans, electricians must follow national safety standards. BS 7671 is the 'Requirements for Electrical Installations,' commonly known as the 'Regs.' Because the full Regs are very detailed, electricians often use the On-Site Guide (OSG), which is a simplified handbook containing the most common rules and tables for everyday installation work.",

      ],

    },

      "objective": "State the specific purpose of different technical information sources per AC 2.1.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Every source of technical information has a specific role in a project. We use Specifications for material selection to ensure the installation meets the quality standards required by the designer. We use Drawings to obtain wiring system information, such as cable routes and equipment positions. Sometimes, you must also consider 'Client Needs.' These are specific requests from the customer that might not be on the main drawings yet, such as a preference for a specific style of decorative faceplate.",
        "Other sources include Guidance Notes, which provide deep technical explanations on complex topics like earthing or protection against electric shock. Finally, the Unite Union Book (also known as the National Working Rules) provides information on site conduct, grading of electricians, and employment conditions. Knowing which source to check allows you to find answers quickly, whether you are looking for a cable route or checking your rights on a construction site."
      ],
      "hint": "Match the source to its job: materials come from specs, routes come from drawings, and specific requests come from the client."
    },

      "title": "Functional Drawing Types",
      "objective": "Distinguish between block diagrams, schematics, and wiring diagrams.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Electricians use different drawing types depending on the task. A Block Diagram is a simple overview using labeled boxes and single lines to show how the main parts of a system relate to each other. It provides the 'big picture' without any detail. A Schematic Diagram shows the logical flow and operation of a circuit. It tells you how the system works and the sequence of components, but it does not show where the parts are physically located in the building.",
        "A Wiring Diagram is much more detailed. It shows the actual physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring between components. If you are trying to understand the logic of a motor starter, you look at the schematic. If you are physically connecting the wires to the terminals inside that starter, you must use the wiring diagram. Confusing these two can lead to wiring errors because a schematic is designed for understanding logic, not for physical installation."
      ],
      "hint": "A schematic shows how a system 'thinks' or works, while a wiring diagram shows exactly where the wires go."

      "title": "As-Fitted Drawings and Bar Charts",
      "objective": "Recognize the purpose of as-fitted drawings and bar charts in project management.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [

        "Once the work is finished, As-Fitted Drawings are produced. These are the final versions of the original design drawings, updated to show exactly what was installed. During construction, cable routes often change to avoid obstacles like new pipes or beams. The as-fitted drawing records these changes accurately. This is vital for future maintenance and safety; if a fault occurs years later, the maintenance electrician relies on the as-fitted drawing to find the exact location of hidden cables or junction boxes without having to tear down walls based on outdated original plans."

      "hint": "One document manages the project's time (Bar Chart), while the other records the final 'as-installed' reality (As-Fitted)."
    }

{
  "terms": [
    {
      "term": "Specifications",
      "simpleDefinition": "A written document detailing the exact materials, quality standards, and installation methods required for a project.",
      "anchor": "The master list for material selection and quality.",
      "misconception": "Thinking specifications are the same as drawings; drawings show where things go, while specifications state what they must be made of."
    },
    {
      "term": "As-fitted Drawings",
      "simpleDefinition": "Technical drawings updated after the work is finished to show the exact final location of all equipment and cables.",
      "anchor": "The 'map' of what was actually installed.",
      "misconception": "Assuming the original design drawings are always accurate; changes often happen during installation that only appear on as-fitted drawings."
    },
    {
      "term": "Schematic Diagram",
      "simpleDefinition": "A drawing that shows the logical flow and operation of a system rather than the physical location of components.",
      "anchor": "Shows how the system works, not where it sits.",
      "misconception": "Trying to use a schematic to find the physical route of a cable through a building."
    },
    {
      "term": "Wiring Diagram",
      "simpleDefinition": "A detailed drawing showing the physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring between components.",
      "anchor": "The guide for making actual physical connections.",
      "misconception": "Confusing a wiring diagram with a schematic; a wiring diagram is for connecting, a schematic is for understanding logic."
    },
    {
      "term": "Block Diagram",
      "simpleDefinition": "A simple overview using labeled boxes to represent main components and lines to show their relationship.",
      "anchor": "The 'big picture' summary of a system.",
      "misconception": "Expecting to see individual wire colors or terminal numbers on a block diagram."
    },
    {
      "term": "Bar Chart",
      "simpleDefinition": "A project management tool used to schedule tasks and track the progress of work over time.",
      "anchor": "The project's timetable.",
      "misconception": "Thinking a bar chart contains technical electrical data; it only manages time and sequence."
    },
    {
      "term": "Manufacturer Data",
      "simpleDefinition": "Technical instructions and limits provided by the equipment maker, such as torque settings or temperature ratings.",
      "anchor": "The specific 'instruction manual' for a product.",
      "misconception": "Believing that general regulations (BS 7671) always override specific manufacturer installation instructions."
    },
    {
      "term": "BS 7671 / On-Site Guide",
      "simpleDefinition": "The national safety standards (Regulations) and the simplified practical handbook for electrical installations.",
      "anchor": "The 'rule book' for safety and compliance.",
      "misconception": "Thinking the On-Site Guide replaces BS 7671; it is a simplified version for common tasks, not a full replacement."
    },
    {
      "term": "Circuit Diagram",
      "simpleDefinition": "A drawing representing the electrical sequence of components in a circuit, often used for fault finding.",
      "anchor": "The electrical path of a single circuit.",
      "misconception": "Thinking a circuit diagram shows the physical layout of the room."
    }

{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "Primary Sources of Technical Information",
      "objective": "Identify the primary sources of technical information used by electricians.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "On any electrical project, you rely on two main site-specific documents: Specifications and Drawings. A Specification is a written document that acts as the master list for the job. Its primary purpose is material selection; it details the exact types, grades, and quality of materials required, such as 'all conduit must be galvanized steel.'",
        "Drawings provide the visual 'wiring system information.' While the specification tells you what to buy, the drawings show you where to put it. They illustrate the physical routes cables must take through a building and the exact locations of accessories like sockets or distribution boards. Using these two sources together ensures you install the correct equipment in the correct place, preventing expensive mistakes or safety issues caused by using the wrong materials for the environment."
      ],
      "hint": "Think about which document is a written list of materials and which is a visual map of the installation."
    },
    {
      "title": "Regulatory and Manufacturer Data",
      "objective": "Explain the role of BS 7671, the On-Site Guide, and manufacturer data.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Beyond site-specific plans, electricians must follow national safety standards. BS 7671 is the 'Requirements for Electrical Installations,' commonly known as the 'Regs.' This is the primary rulebook for electrical safety and compliance in the UK. Because the full Regs are very detailed, electricians often use the On-Site Guide (OSG), which is a simplified handbook containing the most common rules and tables for everyday installation work.",
        "You must also consult Manufacturer Data. This is the specific instruction manual provided by the maker of a component, such as a circuit breaker or a thermostat. It contains critical technical limits, like the exact torque setting for tightening a terminal screw. Following manufacturer data is essential because it ensures the component functions as designed; ignoring a specific torque setting can lead to loose connections and fire risks, even if you followed general regulations."
      ],
      "hint": "Consider the difference between a national safety rulebook (BS 7671) and the specific instructions for a single piece of equipment."
    },
    {
      "title": "Purpose of Technical Sources",
      "objective": "State the specific purpose of different technical information sources per AC 2.1.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Every source of technical information has a specific role in a project. We use Specifications for material selection to ensure the installation meets the quality standards required by the designer. We use Drawings to obtain wiring system information, such as cable routes and equipment positions. Sometimes, you must also consider 'Client Needs.' These are specific requests from the customer that might not be on the main drawings yet, such as a preference for a specific style of decorative faceplate.",
        "Other sources include Guidance Notes, which provide deep technical explanations on complex topics like earthing or protection against electric shock. Finally, the Unite Union Book (also known as the National Working Rules) provides information on site conduct, grading of electricians, and employment conditions. Knowing which source to check allows you to find answers quickly, whether you are looking for a cable route or checking your rights on a construction site."
      ],
      "hint": "Match the source to its job: materials come from specs, routes come from drawings, and specific requests come from the client."
    },
    {
      "title": "Functional Drawing Types",
      "objective": "Distinguish between block diagrams, schematics, and wiring diagrams.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Electricians use different drawing types depending on the task. A Block Diagram is a simple overview using labeled boxes and single lines to show how the main parts of a system relate to each other. It provides the 'big picture' without any detail. A Schematic Diagram shows the logical flow and operation of a circuit. It tells you how the system works and the sequence of components, but it does not show where the parts are physically located in the building.",
        "A Wiring Diagram is much more detailed. It shows the actual physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring between components. If you are trying to understand the logic of a motor starter, you look at the schematic. If you are physically connecting the wires to the terminals inside that starter, you must use the wiring diagram. Confusing these two can lead to wiring errors because a schematic is designed for understanding logic, not for physical installation."
      ],
      "hint": "A schematic shows how a system 'thinks' or works, while a wiring diagram shows exactly where the wires go."
    },
    {
      "title": "As-Fitted Drawings and Bar Charts",
      "objective": "Recognize the purpose of as-fitted drawings and bar charts in project management.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Not all technical documents are about the electrical circuit itself. A Bar Chart is a project management tool used for scheduling. It shows the timeline of the project, indicating when specific tasks start and when they must be finished. It helps the electrician coordinate with other trades, like builders or plumbers.",
        "Once the work is finished, As-Fitted Drawings are produced. These are the final versions of the original design drawings, updated to show exactly what was installed. During construction, cable routes often change to avoid obstacles like new pipes or beams. The as-fitted drawing records these changes accurately. This is vital for future maintenance and safety; if a fault occurs years later, the maintenance electrician relies on the as-fitted drawing to find the exact location of hidden cables or junction boxes without having to tear down walls based on outdated original plans."
      ],
      "hint": "One document manages the project's time (Bar Chart), while the other records the final 'as-installed' reality (As-Fitted)."
    }
  ]
}

integrative.objective: Synthesize the use of functional drawings and project records for maintenance and fault-finding.
retrievalTextLines: Effective maintenance and fault-finding require an electrician to look beyond the general regulations. You must be able to navigate the 'logic' of a system while also knowing exactly where the physical cables were placed during the build.

Original design drawings are often inaccurate by the time a project finishes because of site changes, and a simple block diagram provides the 'big picture' but lacks the detail needed to trace a specific circuit or understand its sequence of operation.
integrative.questionText: You are called to a site to repair a complex lighting control system that was modified during its installation two years ago. To work efficiently, you need to understand the logical sequence of how the controllers operate and find the actual, final routes of the cables behind the ceiling panels. Which two specific technical documents must you use to get this information?
answerGuidance: As-fitted drawings (for the final/actual cable routes)
integrative.hint: One document shows how the system 'thinks' or functions logically, while the other is the final record of where the cables were actually installed after site changes.

steps.title: Primary Sources of Technical Information
steps.objective: Identify the primary sources of technical information used by electricians.
anchorFacts: On any electrical project, you rely on two main site-specific documents: Specifications and Drawings. A Specification is a written document that acts as the master list for the job. Its primary purpose is material selection; it details the exact types, grades, and quality of materials required, such as 'all conduit must be galvanized steel.'

Drawings provide the visual 'wiring system information.' While the specification tells you what to buy, the drawings show you where to put it. They illustrate the physical routes cables must take through a building and the exact locations of accessories like sockets or distribution boards. Using these two sources together ensures you install the correct equipment in the correct place, preventing expensive mistakes or safety issues caused by using the wrong materials for the environment.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking that all technical information is found in a single document.
Think about which document is a written list of materials and which is a visual map of the installation.

steps.title: Regulatory and Manufacturer Data
steps.objective: Explain the role of BS 7671, the On-Site Guide, and manufacturer data.
anchorFacts: Beyond site-specific plans, electricians must follow national safety standards. BS 7671 is the 'Requirements for Electrical Installations,' commonly known as the 'Regs.' This is the primary rulebook for electrical safety and compliance in the UK. Because the full Regs are very detailed, electricians often use the On-Site Guide (OSG), which is a simplified handbook containing the most common rules and tables for everyday installation work.

You must also consult Manufacturer Data. This is the specific instruction manual provided by the maker of a component, such as a circuit breaker or a thermostat. It contains critical technical limits, like the exact torque setting for tightening a terminal screw. Following manufacturer data is essential because it ensures the component functions as designed; ignoring a specific torque setting can lead to loose connections and fire risks, even if you followed general regulations.
misconceptionsToWatch: Believing manufacturer data overrides BS 7671 safety requirements.

steps.title: Purpose of Technical Sources
steps.objective: State the specific purpose of different technical information sources per AC 2.1.
anchorFacts: Every source of technical information has a specific role in a project. We use Specifications for material selection to ensure the installation meets the quality standards required by the designer. We use Drawings to obtain wiring system information, such as cable routes and equipment positions. Sometimes, you must also consider 'Client Needs.' These are specific requests from the customer that might not be on the main drawings yet, such as a preference for a specific style of decorative faceplate.

Other sources include Guidance Notes, which provide deep technical explanations on complex topics like earthing or protection against electric shock. Finally, the Unite Union Book (also known as the National Working Rules) provides information on site conduct, grading of electricians, and employment conditions. Knowing which source to check allows you to find answers quickly, whether you are looking for a cable route or checking your rights on a construction site.
misconceptionsToWatch: Assuming 'Client needs' are always captured fully in the standard drawings.
Match the source to its job: materials come from specs, routes come from drawings, and specific requests come from the client.

steps.title: Functional Drawing Types
steps.objective: Distinguish between block diagrams, schematics, and wiring diagrams.
anchorFacts: Electricians use different drawing types depending on the task. A Block Diagram is a simple overview using labeled boxes and single lines to show how the main parts of a system relate to each other. It provides the 'big picture' without any detail. A Schematic Diagram shows the logical flow and operation of a circuit. It tells you how the system works and the sequence of components, but it does not show where the parts are physically located in the building.

A Wiring Diagram is much more detailed. It shows the actual physical connections and terminal-to-terminal wiring between components. If you are trying to understand the logic of a motor starter, you look at the schematic. If you are physically connecting the wires to the terminals inside that starter, you must use the wiring diagram. Confusing these two can lead to wiring errors because a schematic is designed for understanding logic, not for physical installation.
misconceptionsToWatch: Confusing a schematic diagram with a physical wiring diagram.
A schematic shows how a system 'thinks' or works, while a wiring diagram shows exactly where the wires go.

steps.title: As-Fitted Drawings and Bar Charts
steps.objective: Recognize the purpose of as-fitted drawings and bar charts in project management.
anchorFacts: Not all technical documents are about the electrical circuit itself. A Bar Chart is a project management tool used for scheduling. It shows the timeline of the project, indicating when specific tasks start and when they must be finished. It helps the electrician coordinate with other trades, like builders or plumbers.

Once the work is finished, As-Fitted Drawings are produced. These are the final versions of the original design drawings, updated to show exactly what was installed. During construction, cable routes often change to avoid obstacles like new pipes or beams. The as-fitted drawing records these changes accurately. This is vital for future maintenance and safety; if a fault occurs years later, the maintenance electrician relies on the as-fitted drawing to find the exact location of hidden cables or junction boxes without having to tear down walls based on outdated original plans.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking as-fitted drawings are identical to the original design drawings.
One document manages the project's time (Bar Chart), while the other records the final 'as-installed' reality (As-Fitted).

steps.title: Worked Example: Selecting the Correct Technical Source
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to select and use the correct technical sources when installing a specific piece of equipment, such as a smart thermostat.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are tasked with installing a new smart thermostat in a residential property. You have the thermostat in its box, a set of site drawings, and your On-Site Guide.

Step 1: Consult the On-Site Guide (BS 7671). You check the OSG to ensure the circuit supplying the heating system has the correct overcurrent protection and that your cable selection meets general safety regulations for the environment.

Step 2: Check the Project Specification. You refer to the written specification to confirm the exact model and finish required by the designer (e.g., 'Smart Thermostat - Polished Chrome finish') to ensure the materials you are about to install match the contract.

Step 3: Consult the Manufacturer Data. You open the manufacturer's instruction leaflet to find the specific torque setting for the terminal screws (e.g., 0.5 Nm) and the internal wiring logic for the 'Call for Heat' terminals. This information is not in the Regs or the site drawings.

Step 4: Use the Wiring Diagram. You look at the detailed wiring diagram provided for the heating system to identify which physical wire colors in the wall box correspond to the power supply and the boiler control loop.
